params.text:
From restriction to deprivation of liberty

There is a point where restrictions on a person become so great that they amount to a deprivation of liberty — and a deprivation of liberty must be authorised in law, or it breaches the person’s human rights (Article 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights).

The Supreme Court gave us a simple way to recognise it, known as the ‘acid test’. A person is deprived of their liberty if both of the following apply:

  • They are under continuous (or complete) supervision and control; and
  • They are not free to leave.

Two important points follow. First, it makes no difference that the placement is a ‘good’ one, that the person is content, or that they are not trying to leave. Second, the person’s compliance or lack of objection does not stop it being a deprivation of liberty. If the acid test is met and the person cannot consent because they lack capacity, it must be authorised.

The Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS)

For people in care homes and hospitals, the Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) provide the legal process for authorising a deprivation of liberty. (Deprivations in other settings, such as supported living or a person’s own home, are authorised instead by the Court of Protection.)

How it works:

  • The care home or hospital — the managing authority — must apply to the local authority — the supervisory body — for authorisation.
  • Before it can be granted, the person is assessed against six qualifying requirements: age, mental health, mental capacity, best interests, eligibility (under the Mental Health Act), and no refusals (no valid conflicting advance decision or attorney/deputy decision).
  • If authorised, the person must have a Relevant Person’s Representative to support them, and the right to challenge the authorisation in the Court of Protection. An authorisation is time-limited (up to a maximum of 12 months) and must be reviewed.

Note: the Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS) were passed to replace DoLS but have not been brought into force. DoLS remains the law you must follow.

params.question: A resident with advanced dementia lives on a locked dementia unit. She is happy, never asks to leave, and is checked on constantly by staff. Does this arrangement meet the acid test for a deprivation of liberty?

answers.text: Yes — she is under continuous supervision and control and is not free to leave, so it meets the acid test regardless of how content she is.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Both limbs of the acid test are met. Her contentment and lack of objection are irrelevant — it is still a deprivation of liberty and needs a DoLS authorisation.

answers.text: No — because she is happy and never tries to leave, she is not being deprived of her liberty.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. A person’s compliance or contentment does not remove a deprivation of liberty. If both limbs of the acid test are met, it must be authorised.

answers.text: No — a good, caring placement can never be a deprivation of liberty.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The quality of the placement is irrelevant to the acid test. Even excellent care can amount to a deprivation of liberty that must be lawfully authorised.

answers.text: Only if her family formally object to the locked unit.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. It does not depend on anyone objecting. The acid test is met by the objective facts of continuous supervision and control and not being free to leave.
